beats.text: Fine-tuning is the third tool you reach for, not the first, after prompting and retrieval have failed.

visual.sub: The escalation ladder, in order of effort

beats.text: The ladder runs from cheapest to most expensive: prompting changes nothing, RAG changes context, fine-tuning changes the weights.
The sharpest distinction is knowledge versus behavior: RAG fixes facts that move, fine-tuning bakes a pattern into parameters.
Apply the one-line test: if pasting the perfect paragraph would solve it, the gap is knowledge, not behavior.
Most teams find prompting plus RAG covers about eighty percent of cases before any tuning is justified.

visual.label: of cases covered by prompt + RAG

beats.text: A fine-tune can let a smaller model match a larger one, turning a 70B job into a 3B one.
Once you decide to tune, the dataset is the project; the method is a solved ten-line affair.
Supervised fine-tuning is next-token prediction over examples, with the loss masked to the assistant tokens only.

visual.note: Cross-entropy scored only over completion tokens

beats.text: The LIMA result is the canonical evidence: one thousand curated examples beat far larger but noisier sets.
Quality sets the ceiling: gain rises fast then flattens, and mediocre data never reaches a high-quality model.
The most common bug is the wrong chat template: train and serve with different delimiters and output is garbage.
For style adaptation a few hundred clean examples suffice; a genuine new skill needs low thousands.
LoRA is what makes owning the weights affordable on hardware you actually have.

visual.sub: Low-rank updates on a single card

beats.text: Full fine-tuning with AdamW costs roughly sixteen bytes per parameter, so a 7B model wants about 112 GB before activations.

visual.label: GB optimizer state for full 7B fine-tune

beats.text: LoRA freezes the pretrained weight and learns the update as a product of two thin matrices.

visual.note: B starts at zero, so training begins at the pretrained model

beats.text: At d equal to 4096 and rank 8, the adapter trains 65,536 parameters, about 0.39 percent of the matrix.

visual.label: trainable fraction of the full update

beats.text: Trainable parameters grow linearly in rank while the full update stays fixed, so doubling rank barely moves the fraction.
QLoRA freezes the base in 4-bit NF4, so a 70B model is just 35 GB of frozen weights on one card.
A fine-tune is done when it measurably beats the model you started from, not when the loss looks good.

visual.sub: Beat the base, break nothing else

beats.text: Past a couple of epochs training loss actively lies: it falls while real quality drops as the model overfits.
A practical evaluation has three legs: the target metric, a forgetting probe, and human eyes.
Read fifty outputs per checkpoint, because automated metrics miss tone, format drift, and the fluent-but-wrong failure.
The loop is data, train, eval, fix the data, since most failures are holes in the dataset, not learning rates.
The four classic failures are wrong chat template, eval contamination, overfitting at epoch three, and silent capability regression.
A LoRA fine-tune leaves you a deploy-time choice: fold the adapter into the base or keep it separate.

visual.sub: Merge for speed, separate for scale

beats.text: Merge the adapter once for zero inference overhead, served by any engine exactly like the base model.

visual.note: Merge high, quantize after, to avoid quality loss

beats.text: Keep adapters separate and one GPU can host hundreds of personalities, swapping tiny matrices in and out.
A rank-16 adapter is a few hundred megabytes against tens of gigabytes for the model it steers.
A merged 4-bit 7B fine-tune is the same 3.5 GB and 200 tokens per second as its base.
Licenses bind the fine-tune too: the base model's terms flow through to your derivative, so check before shipping.
